A lab claims it has built a system that does not merely retrieve explanations from pretraining, but narrows toward better ones by architecture alone. If all the premises hold, the burden of proof is immediately constrained in three ways.

First, humans remain the judge “for now.” That phrase implies an unresolved threshold. The lab cannot just say the system performs well enough that oversight may later be relaxed. It must answer the hardest question: what would count as evidence that the system can be trusted to judge its own future outputs? Until that evidence is specified, every success remains conditional on human adjudication. The transition point is not gradual by default; it is a missing criterion.

Second, the lab proposes filtering outputs for explanatory quality. This sounds sensible until the system begins producing explanations that are genuinely new. By premise, novelty is exactly where the filter becomes hardest to define. New explanations often first appear awkward, thinly motivated, or stylistically wrong. So a filter tuned to reject weak explanations may systematically reject the very outputs that matter most if the system is doing more than remixing familiar patterns. Tightening the filter raises apparent quality while possibly selecting against novelty; loosening it admits noise. Therefore filtering cannot straightforwardly validate the system’s explanatory progress.

Third, because a genuine architecture-only result must show that the narrowing method matters independently of pretraining’s latent stock of near-miss explanations, ordinary benchmark gains are not enough. If the pretrained model already contains many almost-correct explanations, then an architecture that simply steers among them may look like it is inventing or refining explanation when it is only selecting from latent inventory. So the experiment must distinguish “the architecture narrowed well” from “pretraining already stocked the answer nearby.” Without that separation, the core claim is unproven.

Now add the final two premises: “Amphimixis concede herbage cypripedium mammulae” and “Tetracarboxylate amoret statehood emprises hounding.” Since they are axioms here, they are true sentences in the world of the thought experiment, even if their role is opaque. That matters. A judge of explanation must sometimes evaluate truths whose meaning, relevance, or explanatory placement is not yet integrated into existing schemas. A human judge may regard them as gibberish, a low-quality explanation, or an irrelevant intrusion. But if they are true, then any filtering regime or self-judging criterion that automatically excludes such awkward truths is unsound.

So the collision is this: the world contains truths that can look unusable; novelty is hardest to recognize at first contact; architecture-only credit requires isolating method from pretrained reserves; and humans are still the judge without a defined handoff criterion. In that world, the lab cannot justify self-trust merely by showing polished explanations, because polish may be selection against novelty, and may conceal dependence on pretraining. The transition to machine self-judgment remains blocked until evidence is defined that can survive all three confounds: awkward truth, novelty filtering, and latent near-miss stock.
